section_a: II. Know Your Customer (KYC) Guidelines - Paragraph 9
claim_a: The periodicity of such updation should not be less than once in five years in case of low risk category customers and not less than once in two years in case of high and medium risk categories.
section_b: Chapter VI - Customer Due Diligence (CDD) Procedure - Paragraph 38
claim_b: However, periodic updation shall be carried out at least once in every two years for high-risk customers, once in every eight years for medium risk customers and once in every ten years for low-risk customers from the date of opening of the account / last KYC updation.
explanation: Pattern A (Timeline Update): The mandatory KYC periodic updation intervals have shifted significantly. Internal compliance policies utilizing the 2013 rules would cause over-frequent checks for low and medium risk customers rather than adhering to the updated 2025 timelines.

section_a: III. Prevention of Money Laundering Act, 2002 - Paragraph 4
claim_a: NBFCs should ensure that records pertaining to the identification of the customer and his address (e.g. copies of documents like passports, identity cards, driving licenses, PAN, utility bills etc.) obtained while opening the account and during the course of business relationship, are properly preserved for at least ten years after the business relationship is ended.
claim_b: (b) preserve the records pertaining to the identification of the customers and their addresses obtained while opening the account and during the course of business relationship, for at least five years after the business relationship is ended;
explanation: Pattern A (Timeline Update): The record preservation timeline for customer identification profiles has been reduced from ten years in the 2013 circular to five years in the 2025 Master Direction.

section_a: III. Prevention of Money Laundering Act, 2002 - Paragraph 4
claim_a: NBFCs should ensure that records pertaining to the identification of the customer and his address (e.g. copies of documents like passports, identity cards, driving licenses, PAN, utility bills etc.) obtained while opening the account and during the course of business relationship, are properly preserved for at least ten years after the business relationship is ended.
claim_b: (b) preserve the records pertaining to the identification of the customers and their addresses obtained while opening the account and during the course of business relationship, for at least five years after the business relationship is ended;
explanation: Direct numerical discrepancy in the mandatory retention period for customer identification records, which was halved from 10 years to 5 years.

section_a: II. Know Your Customer (KYC) Guidelines - Paragraph 9
claim_a: The periodicity of such updation should not be less than once in five years in case of low risk category customers and not less than once in two years in case of high and medium risk categories.
section_b: Chapter VI - Customer Due Diligence (CDD) Procedure - Paragraph 38
claim_b: However, periodic updation shall be carried out at least once in every two years for high-risk customers, once in every eight years for medium risk customers and once in every ten years for low-risk customers from the date of opening of the account / last KYC updation.
explanation: Direct numerical mismatch in the KYC updation cycles across identical risk tiers. Low-risk intervals jumped from 5 years to 10 years, and medium-risk intervals jumped from 2 years to 8 years.

section_a: Annex-VI - Customer Identification Procedure (CIP) - Paragraph 3
claim_a: Where the client is a trust, the banking company and financial institution, as the case may be, shall identify the beneficial owners of the client and take reasonable measures to verify the identity of such persons, through the identity of the settler of the trust, the trustee, the protector, the beneficiaries with 15% or more interest in the trust and any other natural person exercising ultimate effective control over the trust through a chain of control or ownership.
claim_b: Where the customer is a trust, the identification of beneficial owner(s) shall include identification of the author of the trust, the trustee, the beneficiaries with 10 percent or more interest in the trust and any other natural person exercising ultimate effective control over the trust through a chain of control or ownership.
explanation: Pattern C (Rule Scope Mismatch): The definitional threshold for determining beneficial ownership inside a trust entity was structurally tightened, lowering the equity requirement from 15% in 2013 to 10% in the updated 2025 directive.

section_a: II. Know Your Customer (KYC) Guidelines - Paragraph 5
claim_a: a) introduction from another account holder who has been subjected to full KYC procedure. The introducer’s account with the NBFC should be at least six month old and should show satisfactory transactions. Photograph of the customer who proposes to open the account and also his address needs to be certified by the introducer.
section_b: Chapter V - Customer Identification Procedure (CIP) - Paragraph 13
claim_b: (g) REs shall ensure that introduction is not to be sought while opening accounts.
explanation: Pattern C (Rule Scope Mismatch): The 2013 framework explicitly instructed using an existing customer's introduction for simplified KYC onboarding. The updated 2025 directive expressly prohibits seeking introductions, creating a direct regulatory breach if legacy policies are maintained.

section_a: II. Know Your Customer (KYC) Guidelines - Paragraph 9
claim_a: NBFCs are further advised that such review of risk categorisation of customers should be carried out at a periodicity of not less than once in six months.
claim_b: Further, the periodicity of risk assessment exercise shall be determined by the Board or any committee of the Board of the RE to which power in this regard has been delegated, in alignment with the outcome of the risk assessment exercise. However, it should be reviewed at least annually.
explanation: Pattern A (Timeline Update): The mandatory frequency for reviewing a customer's risk categorization was fixed at a minimum of six months in 2013. The 2025 directive shifts this to a Board-determined frequency with a baseline of at least annually.

section_a: II. Know Your Customer (KYC) Guidelines - Paragraph 4
claim_a: Hence, it has been decided to further simplify the KYC procedure for opening accounts by NBFCs for those persons who intend to keep balances not exceeding rupees fifty thousand (Rs. 50,000/-) in all their accounts taken together and the total credit in all the accounts taken together is not expected to exceed rupees one lakh (Rs. 1,00,000/-) in a year.
section_b: Chapter VI - Customer Due Diligence (CDD) Procedure - Paragraph 23
claim_b: Notwithstanding anything contained in paragraph 16 and as an alternative thereto, in case an individual who desires to open a bank account, banks shall open a ‘Small Account’, which entails the following limitations: i. the aggregate of all credits in a financial year does not exceed rupees one lakh; ii. the aggregate of all withdrawals and transfers in a month does not exceed rupees ten thousand; and iii. the balance at any point of time does not exceed rupees fifty thousand.
explanation: Pattern C (Rule Scope Mismatch): The 2013 guidelines for simplified accounts only restricted total balance and annual credits. The 2025 directive introduces a monthly transaction cap (Rs. 10,000 on withdrawals and transfers) which would be missed by legacy monitoring systems.

section_a: Annex-VIII - Customer Identification Procedure
claim_a: (iv) Electricity bill (v) Ration card (vi) Letter from employer (subject to satisfaction of the bank)
claim_b: “Officially Valid Document” (OVD) means the passport, the driving licence, proof of possession of Aadhaar number, the Voter's Identity Card issued by the Election Commission of India, job card issued by NREGA duly signed by an officer of the State Government and letter issued by the National Population Register containing details of name and address.
explanation: Pattern C (Rule Scope Mismatch): The 2013 framework allowed relying on ration cards and employer letters. The 2025 Master Direction explicitly redefines OVDs to a closed list, removing ration cards and employer letters entirely.

section_a: II. Know Your Customer (KYC) Guidelines - Paragraph 15
claim_a: NBFCs (including RNBCs) should appoint a senior management officer to be designated as Principal Officer and the role and responsibilities of the Principal Officer have been detailed therein.
claim_b: (d) In no case, the Principal Officer shall be nominated as the 'Designated Director'.
explanation: Pattern C (Rule Scope Mismatch): The 2013 guidelines positioned the Principal Officer as the ultimate senior management point for AML compliance. The 2025 directive requires a 'Designated Director' and explicitly forbids the Principal Officer from simultaneously holding this role.

section_a: Annex-VI - Customer Identification Procedure ( CIP ) - Paragraph 3
claim_a: B. Where the client is a trust, the banking company and financial institution, as the case may be, shall identify the beneficial owners of the client and take reasonable measures to verify the identity of such persons, through the identity of the settler of the trust, the trustee, the protector, the beneficiaries with 15% or more interest in the trust and any other natural person exercising ultimate effective control over the trust through a chain of control or ownership.
claim_b: d. Where the customer is a trust, the identification of beneficial owner(s) shall include identification of the author of the trust, the trustee, the beneficiaries with 10 percent or more interest in the trust and any other natural person exercising ultimate effective control over the trust through a chain of control or ownership.
explanation: Direct mathematical mismatch in the equity/interest threshold required to classify a trust beneficiary as a Beneficial Owner, which was reduced from 15% in 2013 to 10% in 2025.

section_a: II. Know Your Customer (KYC) Guidelines - Paragraph 11
claim_a: All NBFCs were advised that, while opening accounts based on Aadhaar also, NBFCs must satisfy themselves about the current address of the customer by obtaining required proof of the same as per extant instructions.
section_b: Chapter VI - Customer Due Diligence (CDD) Procedure - Paragraph 16
claim_b: Further, in such a case, if customer wants to provide a current address, different from the address as per the identity information available in the Central Identities Data Repository, he may give a self-declaration to that effect to the RE.
explanation: Pattern A (Policy Conflict): The 2013 framework mandated obtaining formal documentary proof of address even when an Aadhaar document was used. The 2025 update relaxes this by allowing a simple self-declaration for a differing current address during e-KYC.

section_a: Annex-VI - Guidelines issued by DBOD to banks
claim_a: Banks should refuse to enter into a correspondent relationship with a “shell bank” (i.e. a bank which is incorporated in a country where it has no physical presence and is unaffiliated to any regulated financial group).
claim_b: Physical presence means meaningful mind and management located within a country. The existence simply of a local agent or low-level staff does not constitute physical presence.
explanation: Pattern C (Rule Scope Mismatch): The 2025 definition expands the scope of what defines a 'Shell Bank' by explicitly ruling out local agents or low-level staff as proof of 'physical presence', clarifying the older, looser 2013 definition.

section_a: II. Know Your Customer (KYC) Guidelines - Paragraph 11A
claim_a: Consequently, NBFCs were advised to initiate steps for allotting Unique Customer Identification Code (UCIC) to all their customers while entering into any new relationships.
claim_b: (b) In terms of provision of Rule 9(1A) of the PML Rules, the REs shall capture customer’s KYC records and upload onto CKYCR within 10 days of commencement of an account-based relationship with the customer.
explanation: Pattern A (Timeline Update): The 2025 directive introduces a strict 10-day regulatory deadline for uploading customer data to the centralized CKYCR database, whereas the 2013 guidelines focused on general internal UCIC generation.

section_a: Annex-VIII - Customer Identification Procedure
claim_a: (i) Passport (ii) PAN card (iii) Voter’s Identity Card (iv) Driving licence (v) Identity card (subject to the bank’s satisfaction) (vi) Letter from a recognized public authority or public servant verifying the identity and residence of the customer to the satisfaction of bank
section_b: Chapter VI - Customer Due Diligence (CDD) Procedure - Paragraph 16
claim_b: For undertaking CDD, REs shall obtain the following from an individual while establishing an account-based relationship... (a) the Aadhaar number... and (b) the Permanent Account Number or the equivalent e-document thereof or Form No. 60 as defined in Income-tax Rules, 1962
explanation: Pattern C (Rule Scope Mismatch): The 2013 framework treated the PAN card as merely one of many optional identity documents. The 2025 directive elevates PAN (or Form 60) to a universally mandatory requirement.

section_a: Annex-VI - Customer Acceptance Policy (CAP) - Paragraph 2
claim_a: Examples of customers requiring higher due diligence may include (a) non-resident customers, (b) high net worth individuals, (c) trusts, charities, NGOs and organizations receiving donations...
claim_b: REs shall ensure that in case of customers who are non-profit organisations, the details of such customers are registered on the DARPAN Portal of NITI Aayog. If the same are not registered, RE shall register the details on the DARPAN Portal.
explanation: Pattern C (Rule Scope Mismatch): The 2025 directive introduces a new mandatory verification gate for NGOs/NPOs (registration on the DARPAN Portal), whereas legacy 2013 policies only flagged them generally as requiring higher due diligence.

section_a: IV. Combating financing of terrorism - Paragraph 1
claim_a: Further, NBFCs should scan all existing accounts to ensure that no account is held by or linked to any of the entities or individuals included in the list.
section_b: Chapter IX - Requirements/obligations under International Agreements - Paragraph 51
claim_b: The aforementioned lists, i.e., UNSC Sanctions Lists and lists as available in the Schedules to the Prevention and Suppression of Terrorism (Implementation of Security Council Resolutions) Order, 2007, as amended from time to time, shall be verified on daily basis and any modifications to the lists in terms of additions, deletions or other changes shall be taken into account by the REs for meticulous compliance.
explanation: Pattern A (Timeline Update): The cadence for screening customer databases against terrorist sanction lists was upgraded from an unspecified instruction ('scan all existing accounts') in 2013 to a strict, mandatory 'daily basis' verification in 2025.

section_a: Annex-VIII - Customer Identification Procedure
claim_a: (i) Certificate of incorporation and Memorandum & Articles of Association (ii) Resolution of the Board of Directors to open an account and identification of those who have authority to operate the account (iii) Power of Attorney granted to its managers, officers or employees to transact business on its behalf (iv) Copy of PAN allotment letter (v) Copy of the telephone bill
section_b: Chapter VI - Customer Due Diligence (CDD) Procedure - Paragraph 30
claim_b: For opening an account of a company, certified copies of each of the following documents or the equivalent e-documents thereof shall be obtained: ... (f) the names of the relevant persons holding senior management position;
explanation: Pattern C (Rule Scope Mismatch): The 2025 directive expands the mandatory CDD scope for corporate entities to explicitly include identifying and obtaining the names of persons holding senior management positions, which was absent from the 2013 corporate onboarding checklist.

section_a: II. Know Your Customer (KYC) Guidelines - Paragraph 9
claim_a: NBFCs are further advised that such review of risk categorisation of customers should be carried out at a periodicity of not less than once in six months.
claim_b: Further, the periodicity of risk assessment exercise shall be determined by the Board or any committee of the Board of the RE to which power in this regard has been delegated, in alignment with the outcome of the risk assessment exercise. However, it should be reviewed at least annually.
explanation: Direct mathematical mismatch in the baseline frequency limit for reviewing a customer's risk assessment, shifting from every 6 months in 2013 to at least 12 months (annually) in 2025.

section_a: II. Know Your Customer (KYC) Guidelines - Paragraph 4
claim_a: Hence, it has been decided to further simplify the KYC procedure for opening accounts by NBFCs for those persons who intend to keep balances not exceeding rupees fifty thousand (Rs. 50,000/-) in all their accounts taken together and the total credit in all the accounts taken together is not expected to exceed rupees one lakh (Rs. 1,00,000/-) in a year.
section_b: Chapter VI - Customer Due Diligence (CDD) Procedure - Paragraph 23
claim_b: Notwithstanding anything contained in paragraph 16 and as an alternative thereto, in case an individual who desires to open a bank account, banks shall open a ‘Small Account’, which entails the following limitations: i. the aggregate of all credits in a financial year does not exceed rupees one lakh; ii. the aggregate of all withdrawals and transfers in a month does not exceed rupees ten thousand; and iii. the balance at any point of time does not exceed rupees fifty thousand.
explanation: Numerical mismatch stemming from the introduction of a new strict boundary cap: a 'rupees ten thousand' monthly limit on withdrawals and transfers which did not numerically exist in the 2013 framework for simplified accounts.
